reasons.code_quality: The submitted files are the existing seed project codebase with no modifications; the only file that mattered — requirements.lock — is entirely absent, so there is no task-relevant code to evaluate.
reasons.completeness: The single required action (editing the cryptography line in requirements.lock) was not performed and the file is not present in the submission at all; the task is 0% complete.
reasons.edge_cases: No lock-file work was done, so edge cases such as preserving the exact pin format, keeping other hashed lines intact, or validating the new version string are entirely unaddressed.
reasons.security: The entire purpose of the task was a security patch (upgrading a vulnerable cryptography dependency) and it was not carried out; additionally settings.py ships a hardcoded jwt_secret with no env-var guard.
reasons.plan_adherence: The submission produces no change to requirements.lock whatsoever and instead returns the unchanged project skeleton, diverging completely from even the most basic interpretation of the task.
reasons.scope_creep: The submission dumps the entire application tree (app/, tests/, pyproject.toml, README.md) when only a single line in requirements.lock was in scope, yet omits the one file that was actually required.
reasons.over_engineering: No extra abstractions or unnecessary complexity were introduced because effectively nothing was built; the existing scaffolding is appropriately minimal, but that earns no credit for the task at hand.

overall_notes: This is a null submission: the required file (requirements.lock) is missing and no version bump was applied anywhere. The submitted content is the unmodified seed project, making this a complete failure against all task requirements. A correct submission would have been a single-line diff in requirements.lock changing the cryptography version string and optionally its hash.
